segments.s639-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی آمد is a light-clause: آگاهی ('news, information') is the subject and آمد is the past tense verb 'came/arrived' (news arrived). به ایران marks the destination/recipient 'to Iran'. که is the complementizer 'that', introducing the content clause about the king (شاه).
segments.s639-g2: بیامد is the past singular verbal form 'came' (here reported event: the king arrived). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. قنوج is the place-name Kannauj. خود is a possessive/emphatic 'his/own' qualifying the place. با سپاه = 'with an army' where با is 'with' and سپاه is the accompanying noun 'army'. The clause completes the content introduced by که.
segments.s639-g3: ببستند is a past plural verb 'they bound/tied/strung' (used here for hanging decorations). آذین is the direct object 'decorations'. به راه و به شهر uses repeated به to mark location/direction: 'on/along the road and in the city.' Word order is verb‑object‑oblique, typical of Persian narration.
segments.s639-g4: همی is an older/poetic particle adding emphasis (here reinforcing 'every/each'). هرکس means 'each person' or 'everyone'. از کار برداشت is a compound action: از کار 'from work' and برداشت (past) 'took/removed' — i.e. 'took time off'. بهر is the purpose marker 'for/for the sake of'. The whole is elliptical about what they took time for (the celebrations).
segments.s639-g5: درم (dirham) is the object 'coins/money'. ریختند is the past plural of ریختن 'to pour/scatter' — here 'they scattered/poured out (money)'. از کران تا کران is an idiomatic range 'from edge to edge' (i.e. everywhere). The phrase emphasizes abundance and distribution.
segments.s639-g6: This is a simple list of items given or displayed: هم ... هم ... both/also. از marks the items' source/contents ('of'/'from'). مشک 'musk', دینار 'dinar (money)', and زعفران 'saffron' are enumerated with coordinating و and repeated هم for emphasis.
segments.s639-g7: چو 'when' introduces another temporal clause. آگاه شد is 'became aware' or 'learned' (آگاه adjective/noun + شد auxiliary 'became' = came to know). پور او یزدگرد is an appositive/NP: پور 'son', او 'his/he' (possessive/pronoun referring back), and یزدگرد the proper name; the sequence means 'his son, Yazdgerd'.
segments.s639-g8: سپاه پراگنده را is the definite/known object 'the scattered army' (پراگنده 'scattered' modifies سپاه; را marks the definite object). کرد گرد is a two-word verbal expression: کرد (past of کردن 'to do/make') plus گرد (here part of the verbal compound conveying gathering/bringing together). Together they mean 'he caused/collected' or idiomatically 'he gathered (them) together.'
segments.s639-g25: Subject (جهاندار) comes first. بر تخت is a locative phrase 'on the throne' and بر پای خاست is a verbal phrase meaning 'rose to his feet' or 'stood up'; خاست is the past of خاستن 'to rise'. The repeated preposition بر here has its locative/introducing function ('on/onto') rather than a contrastive sense.
segments.s639-g26: بیاراست functions as a past verb 'he adorned/arranged' (third person). پاکیزه and راست are attributive adjectives modifying گفتار (پاکیزه گفتار راست = 'a clean/pure and true/right speech'); Persian poetry permits this stacked adjectival order and omits any copula — the verb بیاراست applies to the whole noun phrase.
segments.s639-g27: نخست is an adverb 'first/at first'. یاد کردن + از is the idiom 'to remember/mention (someone/something)', so از جهان‌آفرین یاد کرد = 'he remembered/mentioned the world‑creator'; کرد is the past auxiliary verb completing the verbal phrase.
segments.s639-g28: ز (poetic variant of از) = 'from' or 'because of'; وام here means 'debt/obligation' and خرد is 'wisdom', so ز وام خرد = 'from/the debt of wisdom'. گردن آزاد کرد is literal 'freed (the) neck' where گردن (neck) is used figuratively for burden/obligation and آزاد کردن + کرد = 'made free / freed'. Word order places the object (گردن) before the adjective (آزاد) and the verb (کرد).
segments.s639-g29: چنین گفت = 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus'). کز is a poetic variant of که meaning 'that' (introducing a content clause). کردگار جهان = 'the Maker/Creator of the world' is the subject of the following nominal predicate. Note: گفت here is the past verb 'said'; the provided noun senses for گفت do not match this verbal use.
segments.s639-g30: شناسنده is a participial/agent noun 'knower' functioning as predicate; آشکار و نهان are two coordinated adjectives 'manifest (clear) and hidden', giving a common poetic antithesis. The copula 'is' is omitted (understood) in Persian poetic style.
segments.s639-g31: بترسید is a plural imperative/subjunctive 'be afraid (of him) / revere'. The conjunction و links the next command: او را ستایش کنید = 'praise him' where را marks او as the definite object and کنید is the plural imperative 'do' attached to the noun ستایش to form the verbal command 'praise/worship'.
segments.s639-g32: شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'dark night'. پیشش (prepositional adverb + enclitic) = 'before him / in his presence' (پیش + -ش). نیایش کنید is again the verbal construction 'offer prayer / supplicate' with کنید as the polite/plural imperative.
segments.s639-g73: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause; دانا is an adjective 'wise' modifying شاه پیروز بخت; بود is the past copula 'was' (standard past tense), so the phrase reads 'when the wise ... was' with the noun phrase 'شاه پیروز بخت' = 'the victorious/fortunate king'.
segments.s639-g74: بنازد is a literary/poetic verb (from نازیدن) meaning 'to take pride in / to show favor/pride toward' and here functions as the main predicate; بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him'; کشور و تاج و تخت is a coordinated series 'realm and crown and throne' serving as the grammatical subject/topic that 'takes pride in' or 'glorying in' him. The verb's form is archaising/poetic but behaves like a present/habitual predicate following the past temporal clause.
segments.s639-g75: ت + را here together form the object/topic marker 'ترا' (literary/poetic order shown as two tokens), addressing 'you' in the sense of 'your ...'; مردی (here 'manliness') is a nominal quality, coordinated with دانش ('learning/knowledge') and فرهی ('nobility' / cultivated nobility). The conjunction و links the three abstract qualities that are the sentence's theme.
segments.s639-g76: فزون آمد is a compound-like verbal idea 'increased / became greater' (فزون 'greater/more' + آمد 'came') and expresses change of degree; از marks the comparison 'than/out of', and تخت شاهنشهی 'the imperial throne' is the standard of comparison. The word order is typical poetic Persian: theme first, then verb, then comparator.
segments.s639-g77: بزرگی 'greatness' is the first nominal in a series coordinated with دانش 'knowledge/learning' and هم‌نژاد 'of the same birth/lineage'; the initial و and repeated هم emphasize 'also/too' (i.e., 'greatness and also learning and also lineage'). These are qualities being compared or praised.
segments.s639-g78: چو here = 'like/as' (introducing a simile) + تو 'you' gives 'like you'; شاه گیتی 'king of the world' is the subject; ندارد 'has not' is the present/past-stative verb 'does not have' (used here to mean 'there is none'); به یاد 'in memory' or 'remembered' is a frozen locution meaning 'is remembered'. Together: 'No world-king is remembered like you.'
segments.s639-g79: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame; آفرین is a nominal/exclamation 'praise/acclaim' acting as the theme; بر تو 'upon you/for you' marks its target; شد ناگزیر is a verbal phrase 'became inevitable/compelled' (شد = 'became', ناگزیر = 'inevitable'), so the clause means 'praise of you has become inevitable.'
segments.s639-g80: ز ما 'from us' introduces the source of that praise; هر که هستیم is a concessive/indefinite clause 'whoever we are' (هر = 'whichever/ whoever', که = relative particle, هستیم = 'we are'); برنا و پیر lists 'young and old' ( برنا = youthful, پیر = old), giving the range of speakers. The whole is 'from us, whoever we may be, young or old.'
segments.s639-g49: یکی here functions as 'a certain / one' (an indefinite determiner) modifying گنج. خواهم + نهادن is a present-future construction: خواه- (desiderative/auxiliary 'will') + infinitive نهادن 'to place', giving 'I will put/set aside'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from / out of', and داد is the noun/participle used here in the sense of what is given (rendered as 'gift' / 'that which is given'); the phrase means 'I will set aside one treasure out of (what I give)'.
segments.s639-g50: که introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'). باشد is the classical subjunctive/present of بودن used here with a jussive/subjunctive force ('may be' / 'be'). روانم is the possessed noun 'my soul' (روان + enclitic -م). پس از = 'after' (پس + از) governs مرگ 'death'. شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy', so the clause reads 'that my soul may be happy after death.'
segments.s639-g51: برین is the fused form of بر + این 'upon/with respect to this' (used adverbially). نیز‌گر here functions as نیز گر = 'if also / moreover if' introducing a counterfactual conditional. خواست is the past of خواستن 'willed / wished' and یزدان is the subject 'God'; بود is the past auxiliary 'was/would have been', so the clause means 'if God had so willed (it)'.
segments.s639-g52: دل روشن is a nominal phrase 'the heart/inner self bright' (دل + adjective روشن). از here marks the source or cause ('from / because of') and governs بخت 'fate/fortune'; خندان is an adjective 'smiling, cheerful' agreeing with بخت. بود is the past copula, so the clause states 'the heart would have been bright from/with smiling fortune.'
segments.s639-g53: برین is 'بر + این' ('on/onto these' or 'with regard to these'). نیکویها is the plural noun 'good deeds/things' (نیکوی + plural marker -ها). فزایش is a noun 'increase' and کنیم (from کردن) is first-person plural subjunctive/imperative used hortatively ('let us do / let us increase'), so the segment means 'let us increase these good deeds.'
segments.s639-g54: سوی is the preposition 'toward(s) / to'. نیک‌بختی is the noun 'good fortune / felicity'. نمایش is a noun 'display / presentation' and کنیم again is the hortative 'let us do / we will do', so the phrase means 'let us display/present (them) toward good fortune.' The verb is repeated to coordinate the two actions.
segments.s639-g55: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. از is the preposition 'from / of'. لشکر 'army' and کارداران 'managers, officials' form a coordinated object; من 'my' is the first-person pronoun placed after the coordinated nouns to indicate possession ('my army and my officials'). The segment sets up a conditional reference to persons among the speaker's forces and officers.
segments.s639-g56: ز is an alternative form of از 'from'. خویشان 'relatives' coordinated with جنگی سواران 'warlike/war horsemen' (جنگی as adjective + سواران 'riders, horsemen'). Again من 'my' marks possession of the coordinated nouns. Together the phrase continues the conditional enumeration: 'from my kin and my warrior-horsemen.'
segments.s639-g97: سپینود را is the direct object marked by the postposition را placed before the verb; پیش او is a locative/prepositional phrase meaning “in the presence of him” (literally “before him”) and comes before the verb. برد is the past tense verb (here ‘brought’/‘took’), and شاه (the subject) appears after the verb, a common inverted word order in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s639-g98: بیاموختش is an older past-form meaning ‘taught him’ (the clitic -ش marks the third‑person object). The following nouns دین و آیین و راه are the items taught (a conjoined list), linked by و; the verb precedes these nouns here, so the action (‘taught’) governs the series of direct objects.
segments.s639-g99: بشستش is the past verb ‘he washed/cleansed him’ with the enclitic object -ش. به دین functions instrumentally ‘by/with the faith’ (به used as ‘by/with’), and the second به introduces the second instrument (‘with’) before آب پاک (‘pure water’), while و links the two instruments.
segments.s639-g100: ازو دور شد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘departed from him / were removed from him’ (ازو = ‘from him’; دور شد = ‘became distant/was removed’). گرد و زنگار و خاک is a coordinated list of what was removed (here گرد = ‘dust’ as a noun), linked by و.
segments.s639-g101: در تنگ زندانها is a locative phrase ‘in the narrow prisons’ (تنگ qualifies زندانها). باز کرد is the compound past ‘opened’ or ‘set free’ (کرد = ‘did/made’)—the subject is omitted and understood from context, as is common in Persian.
segments.s639-g102: به هرسو is an adverbial phrase ‘to/at every side’ = ‘everywhere’. درم دادن is the verbal noun + infinitive ‘giving dirhams’; آغاز کرد is ‘began’ (آغاز + the auxiliary کرد), so the clause means ‘began to give dirhams everywhere.’ Word order places the adverbial and object phrase before the light verb compound.
segments.s639-g81: هم is an adverb meaning “also/too.” آزادی تو (lit. “your freedom”) is the direct object phrase. به یزدان is a prepositional phrase “to God.” کنيم is first-person-plural present-subjunctive/jussive (classical/poetic use) meaning “we shall / let us / we will (do)”; here it governs the whole clause (“we will entrust/offer”). Word order places object and prepositional phrase before the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s639-g82: دگر functions here as an adverb “again/anew.” پیش + آزادمردان is a prepositional phrase meaning “in the presence of / before the men of honor.” کنيم is the same first‑person plural jussive as above; the object (your freedom) is omitted here but carried over from the previous clause (ellipsis).
segments.s639-g83: برین is the fused preposition + demonstrative (بر + این = “on/upon this”). تخت is the noun “throne.” ارزانیانست is a poetic/perspectival predicate meaning “is bestowed / is given” (passive sense), and شاه is the subject (the predicate comes before the noun here, an inverted/poetic order).
segments.s639-g84: A nominal list of the qualities/gifts with which the throne is given: به داد (“with justice”), و به پیروزی (“and with victory”), و دستگاه (“and with a court/retinue”). The repeated به is a prepositional marker linking each noun to the verb in the previous segment (ارزانیانست).
segments.s639-g85: همه مردگان را is the definite object phrase “all the dead” (را marks the definite object). برآری is a poetic/subjunctive form of برآوردن (to raise/bring up) expressing a wish or potential (“may you raise” / “you shall raise”); ز خاک is the ablative/prepositional phrase “from the earth/dust.” The verb is placed after its object and complements, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s639-g86: This is a list of means/qualities introduced by repeated به: به داد (“by/with justice”), و به بخشش (“and by/with generosity” — بخشش here as the act of giving), به گفتار پاک (“by pure speech/with pure words”). These nouns are linked to the verb in the previous segment; the conjunction و simply links items in the list.
segments.s639-g87: خداوند دارنده is a nominal phrase “God, the possessor/holder” (an epithet). یار تو is “your companion/ally,” and باد is the optative particle “may it be” (classical Persian blessing). The clause thus expresses a wish: “May God, the possessor, be your companion.”
segments.s639-g88: سر اختر is an epithet “the chief/top star” (سر here = chief/top). اندر کنار تو is a locative phrase “at your side / beside you.” باد again is the optative particle “may it be,” so the whole clause is a wish: “May the chief star be at your side.”
segments.s639-g33: 'که' begins a relative clause ('who'); 'او' is the clause subject ('he'). 'داد' is the past tense verb 'gave'. The nouns 'پیروزی و دستگاه' form a compound direct object ('gave victory and dominion/court/establishment'); the verb precedes these objects in this poetic word order.
segments.s639-g34: This is a nominal apposition identifying the giver: 'خداوند تابنده خورشید و ماه' = 'the shining Lord, (the) Sun and Moon.' 'خداوند' is the noun, 'تابنده' is an adjective ('shining/resplendent'), and 'خورشید و ماه' are coordinated nouns in apposition; classical/poetic Persian often strings these without an ezafe.
segments.s639-g35: 'هرانکس که' = 'whoever' (relative/head of clause). 'خواهد' is present tense 'wants/wishes'. The second 'که' introduces the subordinate purpose/result clause and licenses the subjunctive verb 'یابد' (here 'attain/find'); 'بهشت' is the object 'Paradise'. The structure is 'whoever wishes that he attain Paradise'.
segments.s639-g36: 'نگردد' is negative jussive/subjunctive 'let him not turn / should not become' (the poetic negative imperative). 'به گرد' is a prepositional phrase 'toward/around' (here 'toward'), and 'بد و کار زشت' are coordinated nouns 'evil and wicked deed(s)'. Together: 'do not turn toward evil and wicked deeds.'
segments.s639-g37: 'چو' = 'when'. 'داد و دهش باشد' uses 'باشد' (classical subjunctive/coptula) to state existence: 'there be/there is justice and giving (generosity)'. The final 'و راستی' adds 'and uprightness' as another coordinating predicate; so the whole means 'when justice and giving and uprightness exist.'
segments.s639-g38: 'بپیچد' is a present/future-oriented verbal form with the verbal prefix 'ب' meaning 'will/does turn' (here 'will turn/turns'). The subject 'دل' ('the heart') follows the verb (poetic V–S order). 'از کژی و کاستی' means 'away/from crookedness and deficiency' — the phrase marks what the heart turns away from.
segments.s639-g39: 'ز ما کس مباشید' is an exhortation: 'be no one of us' with 'مباشید' the negative imperative (2nd person plural) meaning 'do not be'; here: 'let none of us be'. 'زین پس' is a fixed phrase 'from now on / henceforth' and 'به بیم' ('in fear') completes 'do not be in fear.' So: 'From now on, let none of us be afraid.'
segments.s639-g40: A conditional clause introduced by 'اگر' ('if'). 'کوه زر دارد' = 'a mountain has gold' with 'دارد' as present 'has'. The coordinating 'و گنج سیم' continues the list: 'and a treasure (of) silver' — a poetic concession 'even if a mountain has gold and a hoard of silver.'
segments.s639-g17: This clause is a temporal subordinate: 'چون' (here functioning as 'when') introduces the time clause. 'بیاسود' is an adjectival/participial predicate describing the state (literally 'un-eased' / 'not at rest'); 'گشت' is the past tense verb 'became/turned'; the noun phrase 'گیتی سیاه' (world + adjective) is the subject/complement meaning 'the dark world'. Word order is poetic: predicate/adverbial + temporal 'چون' + verb + subject phrase.
segments.s639-g18: 'به کردار' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the form/likeness of'; 'سیمین' (silvery) qualifies 'سپر' (shield). 'گشت' is again the past-tense intransitive 'became/turned into', and 'ماه' is the subject 'the moon'. The literal order is 'in the form of a silvery shield became the moon' — i.e. 'the moon became like a silvery shield.'
segments.s639-g19: 'چو' here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. 'پیراهن شب' is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the robe of night' (poetic image for darkness). 'بدرید' is the past tense (3rd person) 'was torn' (poetic transitive/pas-sive sense), and 'روز' (day) follows as the main referent that is introduced by this event.
segments.s639-g20: 'پدید آمد' is a two-word verbal complex meaning 'appeared/came into view' (پدید + آمد). 'آن' is the determiner 'that/the', and 'شمع' ('candle') is the noun being described. The following compound epithet 'گیتی فروز' ('world-illumining' / 'world-brightening') qualifies 'شمع', so the whole NP means 'that candle which lights the world' (i.e., the sun).
segments.s639-g21: Simple main clause: 'شهنشاه' (the emperor) is the subject, followed by the prepositional phrase 'بر تخت زرین' ('upon the golden throne'), and the past verb 'نشست' ('sat'). Adjective 'زرین' ('golden') directly modifies 'تخت'.
segments.s639-g22: 'در بار بگشاد' is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning 'he opened the court / held court' (درِ بار = the court/assembly; بگشاد here functions as a past action 'opened'). The conjunction 'و' links the next action 'لب را ببست' where 'لب را' uses the object marker 'را' and 'ببست' (past) literally 'he closed' — idiomatically 'he closed his lips', i.e. was silent. The two clauses are coordinated actions of the king.
segments.s639-g23: Relative clause with 'هر کس که' meaning 'everyone who'. 'برفتند' is a plural past verb 'they departed' (poetic agreement with the plural sense of 'هر کس' as a collective), and 'بد مهتری' is a descriptive phrase indicating elevated rank/position (here 'مهتری' = preeminence/chief). The clause thus reads 'they departed — every person who was of preeminence...'.
segments.s639-g24: This continues the relative description: 'خردمند' = 'wise' modifies the people just mentioned; 'و' links the next phrase 'در پادشاهی سری' where 'در پادشاهی' means 'in the kingship/at court' and 'سری' means 'secret/privy' (i.e. those who had access to the king's inner matters). Together: 'wise and privy to the affairs of the kingdom.'
segments.s639-g89: برفتند is a past third‑person plural verb 'they departed/left' (prefix بر‑ + رفتند); it appears before its subject, a common poetic order. با رامش is an adverbial phrase ('with calmness/peace') modifying the verb. از پیش تخت is a prepositional phrase 'from before the throne / in front of the throne' (از = from, پیش = before/in the presence of, تخت = throne).
segments.s639-g90: بزرگان و فرزانه is the subject in noun phrase form ('the great ones and the wise'); classical Persian often lists such epithets in coordination. نیک‌بخت is an epithet/adjective 'fortunate' attached to them. The whole phrase is in apposition to the action (those who departed).
segments.s639-g91: نشست here is a past verb 'sat/mounted' (sit/settle) used with the following subject; آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' placed after the verb for poetic order. شاه و لشکر is the coordinated subject 'the king and the army/host.' بر اسپ is the locative/instrumental expression 'upon horses' (بر = on/upon, اسپ = steed/horse).
segments.s639-g92: بیامد is a past‑tense verb 'came' (third person). سوی is a directional preposition 'toward.' خان is an honorific/title 'khan/lord' placed before the proper name; آذر گشسپ is a compound proper name (Azar Gushasp). Together the phrase means 'came toward Khan Azar Gushasp.'
segments.s639-g93: بسی is an adverbial quantifier 'much' modifying the following nouns. زر و گوهر is a coordinated direct object 'gold and gems.' به درویش marks the indirect object with the preposition به ('to/for the dervish'). داد is the past verb 'gave.' Note classical order: quantifier + objects + prepositional indirect object + verb.
segments.s639-g94: نیاز here denotes the 'need' or 'supplication' being referred to; آنک (classical relative/pronominal particle) links to the following apposition 'بنهفت' ('that one who was from Benehaft'). ازو functions as an indirect‑object pronoun in this context ('to him/for him'), and بیش داد 'gave more' states that he gave in excess. The clause therefore means he satisfied that person's need and gave him still more.
segments.s639-g95: پرستنده is an agent noun 'worshipper/devotee.' آتش زردهشت is a genitive construction 'the fire of Zardasht' (Zardasht = Zoroaster); together it yields 'a worshipper of Zoroaster's fire' or 'a Zoroastrian fire‑worshipper.' Word order: modifier (پرستنده) + possessed phrase (آتش زردهشت).
segments.s639-g96: همی رفت is a poetic iterative/continuous past ('kept going / proceeded')—همی marks ongoing or habitual action while رفت is past 'went.' با باژ is an instrumental phrase 'with a cry/noise' (با = with, باژ = cry). برسم here is a form meaning 'upon reaching' or 'when he reached' (verbal adjunct of attendant action), followed by به مشت ('with / at the fist' — مشت = fist), so the clause describes him advancing shouting and then striking with his fist.
segments.s639-g41: ز دلها = a prepositional phrase meaning "from the hearts" (ز is the poetic preposition 'from'). همه بیم modifies بیم ("all fear"). بیرون کنید is the second-person plural imperative/subjunctive form of کردن with بیرون ("out, away") after the object: literally "make (the fear) out" = "drive out the fear." Word order is prepositional phrase → quantified object → verb (imperative).
segments.s639-g42: نیایش ("prayer/supplication") is the object; به introduces the indirect object/recipient ("to/for"). دارای here functions poetically as a noun meaning "possessor/owner" (literally "having/possessing") and بیچون is a postpositive adjective "matchless." کنید again is the 2nd-person plural imperative/subjunctive of کردن: "offer/pray to" or "make prayer to."
segments.s639-g43: کشاورز‌گر is a condensed conditional protasis: the suffix -گر here gives an "if" sense ("if a farmer"). مرد دهقان‌نژاد is an appositive nominal phrase "a man of peasant birth." The whole segment supplies the conditional subject/assumption for the following clause.
segments.s639-g44: بکوشید is the 2nd-person plural imperative/subjunctive of کوشیدن ("strive/try"). با ما is the accompanying phrase "with us." به هنگام داد is a time phrase "at the time/hour of داد," where داد here means "justice." The verb comes early as an exhortation followed by the companion and temporal phrase.
segments.s639-g45: هران را که introduces a relative clause: هران ("those/each of them") is marked by را as the definite object; که introduces the relative clause "whom/that." ما تاج دادیم و تخت is the verb phrase "we gave crown and throne" (دادیم = simple past). The clause therefore means "those whom we gave crown and throne."
segments.s639-g46: ز یزدان = "from God" (ز is the poetic preposition "from"). شناسید is an imperative/exhortation (2nd-person plural) of شناختن meaning "recognize/acknowledge." وز here is a poetic fused form functioning as 'و ز' = "and from" (a compact conjunction + preposition used in poetry). داد و بخت are nouns "justice and fate/fortune." Together: "acknowledge them as from God, and from justice and fate."
segments.s639-g47: نکوشم is a first-person singular verb form of کوشیدن with a negative prefix n- here giving the sense "I will not try / I shall not endeavor." به introduces the infinitival/gerund-like verb آگندن ("to soil/taint") as the aim of the action, and گنج من is the direct object "my treasure." The verb form is emphatic, promising non-action regarding tainting the treasure.
segments.s639-g48: نخواهم is the analytic first-person future/volitional with negative prefix: "I will not / I do not wish to." پراگنده is an adjective "scattered," کرد is the light verb "do/make" used here transitively, and انجمن is the object "assembly/association." Together: "I will not scatter the assembly."
segments.s639-g57: کسی (indefinite subject) + رنج بگزید is a compact verbal phrase: رنج (suffering) with the verb بگزید (classical past form built on گزیدن) yields 'endured/underwent suffering' — here the verb is put after its object, a common Persian order. و با من نگفت is a coordinate clause: و 'and' links the clauses, با من 'to/with me' is the indirect object placed before نگفت; نگفت is a negated/past-form of گفتن ('did not tell' or 'did not speak to'), used elliptically (the negative prefix is fused in classical orthography).
segments.s639-g58: همی is a poetic adverb meaning 'still/continually' placed before the verb دارد (present/continuous 'has, harbors'). آن کژی is the demonstrative + noun ('that crookedness/wrongdoing') functioning as the object of دارد. اندر نهفت is a prepositional phrase with archaic اندر ('in/within') and نهفت (a past-participial/adjectival form 'hidden, concealed') modifying کژی; the verb phrase means 'still harbors that fault hidden within.'
segments.s639-g59: ورا is a prepositional/pronominal phrase 'to him/for him' placed at clause start for emphasis. از تن خوی‌ش is an ablative phrase 'from his own body' where تن 'body' is followed by خوی + ش (root + possessive suffix) to express 'his own.' باشد is the present/subjunctive copula ('there is / it will be') and positions the predicate noun بزه ('fault, blame') after it: 'there will be blame to him from his own body.'
segments.s639-g60: This is a rhetorical question. بزه is fronted as the topic ('blame/fault'); کی is the interrogative 'who' (here functioning rhetorically 'who would...'); گزیند is present/factual 'chooses' (گزیدن); کسی is 'anyone' as the logical subject; بی‌مزه is a predicate adjective 'tasteless / dull' attached to کسی. The line reads roughly 'Who would choose blame — anyone tasteless?' with an inversion typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s639-g61: منم is emphatic 'I am' (subject + copula fused). پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of God' placed after the subject for emphasis. ازو is the beneficiary/object pronoun 'for him/of him' that the following predicate دادخواه ('seeker of justice/avenger') relates to: grammatically the structure is 'I am before God (the) دادخواه of him' — i.e. 'I stand before God as his avenger/pleader.'
segments.s639-g62: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying دادخواه ('who ...'). در + noun places location: در چادر ابر is a poetic compound 'in a tent of cloud' (چادر 'tent' qualified by ابر 'cloud' in attributive order). بنهفت ماه is a temporal/place expression: in this diction بنهفت functions as a bound element (here treated as a place/name or archaic time phrase) with ماه ('month') — the whole phrase indicates 'hid/lay within the cloud-tent for months' (an archaic/timebleached expression).
segments.s639-g63: شما را uses شما + the object marker را to topicalize 'you/your'; مگر here functions rhetorically ('is it not / perhaps / is it then') introducing a challenge. دیگرست is the verbal/adjectival predicate 'is other/different' and آرزوی ('wish/desire') is the noun being predicated (object/predicate) — effectively: 'Is your wish then different?'
segments.s639-g64: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause: هرکس 'whoever' is the subject, دگرگونه is the predicate adjective 'different,' باشد is the subjunctive/copular verb ('be' — here stating a general/hypothetical condition), and به خوی is a prepositional phrase 'in/with regard to disposition/nature' modifying دگرگونه. The clause reads 'that whoever is different in disposition.'
segments.s639-g9: 'چو' here is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. The clause lists two arrivals: 'نرسی' (here functioning as 'he arrived' / the arrival of Nersi) and 'موبد موبدان' ('mōbed of mōbeds' = the chief priest) joined by 'و'. Word order is the poetic inverted sequence 'چو X و Y' introducing the time clause.
segments.s639-g10: پذیره شدندش is a poetic way of saying 'they received him' (پذیرفتندش): 'پذیره' (reception/acceptance) plus the plural past 'شدند' with the enclitic object pronoun 'ش' = 'him'. 'همه بخردان' ('all the wise') is the plural subject performing the action. The object pronoun refers back to the person introduced earlier.
segments.s639-g11: 'چو' = 'when'; 'بهرام را' is the definite object (را marks Bahram as the thing seen); 'دید' is the past 'saw'. 'فرزند اوی' is the subject 'his son' (اوی = 'his'), placed after the verb by poetic inversion — the subject of 'دید'.
segments.s639-g12: 'بیامد' = 'he came' (the same subject, 'his son', continues). 'بمالید' = 'he rubbed' or 'pressed' (مالیدن) and shares the same subject; Persian often omits repeating the subject. 'بر خاک روی' = 'his face upon the ground' (بر + خاک = 'on the ground', روی = 'face'); possessive is understood from the context.
segments.s639-g13: 'برادرش' = 'his brother' (ش = his). 'نرسی و موبد همان' lists two persons in coordination: the brother Nersi and 'موبد همان' ('that same mobed' — همان points back to the previously mentioned priest). This is a coordinated nominal phrase describing the two figures.
segments.s639-g14: 'پر از' = 'full of'; 'گرد رخسار' = 'dust of the countenance' i.e. their faces covered with dust; 'و دل شادمان' = 'and (their) hearts joyful'. Possessive markers are omitted but understood to refer to the coordinated subjects in the prior segment.
segments.s639-g15: 'چنان هم' = 'thus/so as well' (چنان = thus, هم = also). 'بیامد' = 'he came/went' and 'به ایوان خویش' = 'to his own iwan/porch' (خویش = his own) — the prepositional phrase gives destination; poetic word order places the verb before the phrase.
segments.s639-g16: 'به یزدان' = 'to God'; 'سپرده' is the past participle 'entrusted' (here meaning 'having entrusted'); 'تن و جان خویش' = 'his body and soul' (خویش = his own). The phrase reads as 'having entrusted his body and soul to God.'
segments.s639-g65: بگویید is the 2nd-person plural imperative (or polite imperative) of 'say/tell', introducing a command. گستاخ functions as a vocative/adjectival label ('impudent/audacious one') addressing the addressee of the command. با من سخن is a prepositional phrase ('speak with me') where با governs من (object pronoun) and سخن is the noun 'speech' (i.e., 'speak'). The word order places the imperative first, then the phrase being ordered.
segments.s639-g66: مگر here means 'unless' (a concessive/exclusive particle). نو کنم is نو + کنم, where کنم is 1st-person present-subjunctive/optative 'I may/might make' (or 'I should make/renew'), so نو کنم = 'I renew/make new'. آرزوی کهن is the object phrase 'old wish' (noun + postpositive adjective کهن). The whole clause expresses a conditional: 'unless I renew the old wish.'
segments.s639-g67: همه addresses everyone ('all, everyone') as vocative. گوش دارید is a present indicative (2nd-person plural) literally 'you have ears' — an idiom meaning 'listen / pay attention'. و links the clauses. فرمان کنید is an imperative (2nd-person plural) with فرمان as the object; here it means 'perform/observe the command' (i.e., 'obey / act on the instruction') — imperative form and object follow the usual Persian ordering.
segments.s639-g68: ازین (از + این) = 'from this' (از = 'from/of', ین is an orthographic variant of این). پند = 'advice/counsel' is the source. آرایش جان کنید is an imperative construction: آرایش = 'adornment/arrangement', جان = 'life/soul', and کنید (2nd-person plural imperative) = 'make/do' — together 'make the adornment of your life' (i.e., 'take this counsel to heart / let it adorn your life'). Note adjective/noun ordering: here آرایش (noun) precedes جان (noun in genitive relation).
segments.s639-g69: بگفت is simple past 3rd-person singular 'he said'. این refers to the just-spoken material ('this' or 'the foregoing'). و is the conjunction 'and'. بنشست is past 3rd-singular 'he sat down'. بر تخت is the locative phrase 'on (the) throne' (بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne'). داد is a past verb 'gave'; in this line it sequences with the following clause (the syntax links two successive actions: speaking/sitting and then giving/placing).
segments.s639-g70: کلاه کیانی is a noun phrase 'the Kayanian cap/crown' (کلاه = hat/cap, کیانی = Kayanian/royal). به سر بر نهاد is a verb phrase 'placed upon the head': به (preposition, 'to/onto'), سر = 'head', بر = 'on/upon' (here reinforcing locative), and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/put' (i.e., 'he placed'). Together: 'he placed the Kayanian hat on (his) head.'
segments.s639-g71: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones' or 'the elders'. برو here is an archaic/poetic adverbial particle modifying خواندند, giving a sense of immediately or loudly (it is not functioning as the common imperative 'go'). خواندند is past simple, 3rd person plural 'they said/sang/called', and آفرین is the quoted interjection they uttered ('Bravo!'). Word order is Subject + adverbial particle + verb + quoted/acclamatory object.
segments.s639-g72: که introduces a subordinate clause (here giving the content of the acclamation). بی‌تو is a prepositional-like phrase 'without you'. مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'lest' and often stands in classical Persian for an omitted verb such as 'باشد' ('may there be not'); thus the verb is elliptic. کلاه و نگین are the coordinated nouns 'crown/hat and gem' — the things asserted should not exist 'without you'.

sentences.s639-s1: When news came to Iran that the king had arrived from Kannauj with an army.
sentences.s639-s2: They hung decorations along the road and in the city; each person took time away from work for (the purpose).
sentences.s639-s3: They showered out dirhams from edge to edge; also (gave) musk and dinars and likewise saffron.
sentences.s639-s4: When his son Yazdgerd learned (of this), he gathered the scattered army together.
sentences.s639-s13: The sovereign rose from his throne to his feet and arranged a pure, true speech.
sentences.s639-s14: First he remembered the Creator of the world, and he freed his neck from the debt of wisdom.
sentences.s639-s15: He said thus that the Maker of the world is the knower of the manifest and the hidden.
sentences.s639-s16: Fear him and praise him; in the dark night, pray before him.
sentences.s639-s37: When the fortunate, victorious king was wise, the realm, crown, and throne took pride in him.
sentences.s639-s38: Your manliness, learning, and nobility grew greater than the imperial throne.
sentences.s639-s39: No king of the world is remembered who, like you, has such greatness, learning, and lineage.
sentences.s639-s40: Now praise of you has inevitably arisen; from us, whoever we are — young or old.
sentences.s639-s25: I will set aside a certain treasure as a gift so that my soul may be happy after death.
sentences.s639-s26: If God had so willed it (otherwise), then the heart would have been bright and fortunate.
sentences.s639-s27: Let us increase these good deeds and present them toward good fortune.
sentences.s639-s28: If (it is) from my army and my officials, from my relatives and my war-horsemen...
sentences.s639-s49: The king brought Sepinoud before him and taught him religion, rites, and the way.
sentences.s639-s50: He washed him in the faith and with pure water; dust, rust, and soil were removed from him.
sentences.s639-s51: He opened the narrow prisons and began giving dirhams everywhere.
sentences.s639-s41: We will also entrust your freedom to God; again we will place it before the men of honor.
sentences.s639-s42: This throne is bestowed upon the king — with justice, with victory, and with a retinue.
sentences.s639-s43: May you raise all the dead from the earth — by justice, by generosity, and by pure speech.
sentences.s639-s44: May God, the Lord and Sustainer, be your companion; may the chief star be at your side.
sentences.s639-s17: who gave victory and dominion — the Lord, the radiant Sun and Moon.
sentences.s639-s18: Whoever wishes to attain Paradise should not turn toward evil and wicked deeds.
sentences.s639-s19: When there is justice, generosity, and uprightness, the heart will turn away from crookedness and deficiency.
sentences.s639-s20: From now on, let no one of us be in fear — even if a mountain has gold and a hoard of silver.
sentences.s639-s9: When the world grew dark and uneasy, the moon became like a silvery shield.
sentences.s639-s10: When the robe of night was torn, day appeared — that world-illumining candle shone forth.
sentences.s639-s11: The king-king (shahanshah) sat upon the golden throne; he opened the court and closed his lips.
sentences.s639-s12: Everyone who was of high rank, wise, and privy in the kingship departed.
sentences.s639-s45: They departed peacefully from before the throne of the great and the wise, the fortunate.
sentences.s639-s46: At that time the king and the host mounted their horses and came toward Khan Azar Gushasp.
sentences.s639-s47: He gave much gold and jewels to the dervish; he fulfilled that one's need — the one from Benehaft — and gave him even more.
sentences.s639-s48: The fire‑worshipper of Zardasht kept coming with a cry and, on reaching, struck with his fist.
sentences.s639-s21: Drive all fear out of hearts; offer prayer to the matchless Possessor.
sentences.s639-s22: If a farmer is a man of peasant birth, strive with us in the hour of justice.
sentences.s639-s23: Recognize those to whom we gave crown and throne as (coming) from God, and from justice and fate.
sentences.s639-s24: I will not try to soil my treasure; I will not scatter the assembly.
sentences.s639-s29: Someone endured suffering and did not tell me; he still harbors that crookedness hidden within.
sentences.s639-s30: To him, from his own body, there will be blame; who would choose blame — anyone tasteless?
sentences.s639-s31: I am, before God, his seeker of justice (his avenger), who lay hidden in a tent of cloud for months.
sentences.s639-s32: Is your wish different — that everyone should be different in disposition?
sentences.s639-s5: When Nersi and the chief priest arrived, all the wise men received him.
sentences.s639-s6: When his son saw Bahram, he came forward and pressed his face to the ground.
sentences.s639-s7: His brother Nersi and that same mobed were with faces covered in dust and hearts rejoicing.
sentences.s639-s8: Thus he also went to his own porch, having entrusted his body and soul to God.
sentences.s639-s33: Tell him to speak boldly to me, unless I should make the old wish new again.
sentences.s639-s34: Everyone, lend your ears and obey; take this counsel and make it the adornment of your life.
sentences.s639-s35: He spoke these words, sat down upon the throne, (and) gave — then placed the Kayanian cap upon his head.
sentences.s639-s36: The elders cried out, "Bravo! May there be no crown or jewel without you."
